I have very generic types of equipment that a bare minimum of fields, and I'm specializing them so that they always have the same fields as the parent. If I was able to define a set of fields for an abstract type the must be available to all sub-types that would make my code adhere to DRY. 

I'm aware from https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24960 that getproperty can be used to overload property access, but if someone Author A defined a required field for an AbstractType but user B wants to subclass it in a way for that field to not be there, user B could simply
1. Build their own constructor that fills that property with a default value
2. Override that property access with the desired behaviour in "getproperty" higher up in the "if statement" block
It seems to me that if a method works on AbstractType and accesses its fields, then getproperty should be defined for a certain set of fields. "getproperty" becomes a sort of interface definition, and that's just how interfaces work (although if you REALLY don't want people to access that field, you can make getproperty return an error). It's certainly less work and less error-prone than copy-pasting the same fields over and over again.

If it turns out that abstract types with fields is a bad idea, would it be acceptable to have a macro that expands getproperty and propertynames that include the properties of a nested object?

author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi, the following is a memory leak:

```julia
x = 1234
while (true)
    GC.enable(false)
    x = rand()
    GC.enable(true)
    #GC.collect(GC.GC_AUTO)
end
```

This slowly fills up the RAM until the OS kills the process. With `#GC.collect(GC.GC_AUTO)` commented in, this code behaves as expected. Without any of the `GC.` calls, no memory leak occurs either.

Clearly the GC is still updating the roots, otherwise `GC.collect` would have no effect. For some reason, the unused `rand()` values are never deallocated. This same behavior is exhibited if implementing the above using the C-API.

Tested on Julia 1.7.2, on Ubuntu 20.04.2 LTS, 64-bit, but I've asked a few people, and they all reproduced it.

I also opened [a discourse thread about this](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/memory-leak-gc-does-not-deallocate-values-allocated-while-it-was-turned-off-unless-specifically-requested/79904)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45068/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45068/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1250419167","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45068#issuecomment-1250419167","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45068","id":1250419167,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5Kh-Hf","user":{"login":"Deduction42","id":40864154,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwODY0MTU0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40864154?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42","html_url":"https://github.com/Deduction42","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Deduction42/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-09-19T00:13:40Z","updated_at":"2022-09-19T00:13:40Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"I In version 2.0.5, it managed to run through the precompiliaion workflow, but when building the incremental system image, there was an issue:

```
could not load symbol "pgamma":
/usr/local/julia/bin/julia: undefined symbol: pgamma
Stacktrace:
  [1] gammacdf
    @ ~/.julia/packages/StatsFuns/mQJB7/src/rmath.jl:83 [inlined]
  [2] cdf
    @ ~/.julia/packages/Distributions/O4ZJg/src/univariates.jl:622 [inlined]
```
This looks like a problem generated by the macro in this file, line 83 where the actual symbol couldn't be loaded
https://github.com/JuliaStats/StatsFuns.jl/blob/master/src/rmath.jl

I would almost say it was a problem with the StatsFuns.jl package, except that PackageCompiler.jl v1.2.5 was able to handle it.
